quoteLast: , Bernard Hertzog, having returned to Saverne, was seated before his writing-desk, and describing in his chapter upon the antiquities of the Dagsberg, his discovery of the Merovingian arms in the woodman's hut in the Nideck. Then he went on to prove that the name of Tribocci, or Triboques, was derived from the German _drei büchen_--that is, three beeches.
title: The Man-Wolf and Other Tales
author: Erckmann-Chatrian

quoteTime: About six o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: of the next day, when the boys at “Fifteen-Two” were finding room for their legs under the long pine tables spread with an imposing array of cookies, doughnuts, hot biscuit, fried ham, potatoes, jam, and pies, Slocum, stumbling through the doorway, paused in the shadow cast by the lamps.
title: Lost Farm Camp
author: Knibbs, Henry Herbert, 1874-1945; Cue, Harold James, 1887-1961 [Illustrator]

quoteLast: we reached Kapit. The Fort stands on a hill, and steps cut out in the sharp, steep banks lead up to its front door. It stands some forty feet above the level of ordinary tides, but in the rainy season, when heavier freshets than those in the fine season collect up river, the water has been known to reach several feet above the flooring of the Fort.
title: My life in Sarawak
author: Brooke, Margaret, Lady, 1849-1936; Swettenham, Frank Athelstane, Sir, 1850-1946 [Author of introduction, etc.]

quoteFirst: Whereupon the tempter within me said--'And, now you have flung down the pen, you may as well fling yourself out of the window; what remains for you to do?'  Why, to take it up again, thought I to myself, for I did not like the latter suggestion at all--and then forthwith I resumed the pen, and wrote with greater vigour than before, from
quoteTime: about six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: until I could hardly see, when I rested for a while, when the tempter within me again said, or appeared to say--'All you have been writing is stuff, it will never do--a drug--a mere drug'; and methought these last words were uttered in the gruff tones of the big publisher.
title: Lavengro: The Scholar, the Gypsy, the Priest
author: Borrow, George, 1803-1881; Birrell, Augustine, 1850-1933 [Commentator]; Sullivan, Edmund J. (Edmund Joseph), 1869-1933 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: , passed through Snickersville on a narrow stony road, and arrived at Snicker's Gap about noon. We went through the gap, but on arriving at the Shenandoah river at Island Ford
quoteLast: found that some of Crook's force had crossed and was skirmishing; did not fight very well; fell back to the river in a stampede, plunged in and some were drowned; probably green troops. Mosby's guerillas have been in our rear all day and robbed some of our stragglers.
title: Personal Recollections and Civil War Diary, 1864
author: Abbott, Lemuel Abijah, 1842-1911

quoteFirst: An engagement, lasting from five o'clock P. M. till dark, was going on at Chantilly amidst a heavy thunder shower. Generals Kearney and Stevens were killed.
title: Diary of Battery A, First Regiment Rhode Island Light Artillery
author: Reichardt, Theodore

quoteFirst: Westerman had obtained guides to show him the road to Clisson. It was
quoteTime: about six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: when the advanced portion of his army, consisting of three thousand men, had proceeded about a league from Amaillou. He was himself riding nearly at the front of the column, talking to his aide-de-camp and one of the guides, when he was startled by hearing a noise as of disturbed branches in the hedge, only a few feet in advance of the spot in which he was standing; he had not, however, time to give an order, or speak a word on the subject, before a long sudden gleam of fire flashed before his eyes; it was so near to him that it almost blinded him: a cannon had been fired off close to his
title: La Vendée
author: Trollope, Anthony, 1815-1882

quoteFirst: Rowton, who had busied himself all day seeing to his guests and hastening their departure, came into the room where his wife and Lady Georgina were seated,
quoteTime: about six o’clock in the evening
title: A Son of Ishmael: A Novel
author: Meade, L. T., 1854-1914; Shute, A. B. (Augustus Burnham), 1851-1906 [Illustrator]

quoteTime: a little after six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , as near as I can remember, when I returned to my cottage after having visited my wireless apparatus, and made sure that no messages were being received. I had barely entered my room when Squire Treherne paid me a visit.
title: The Passion for Life
author: Hocking, Joseph, 1860-1937
